agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A logistics/courier platform operating in EU and UK markets proposes reducing delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days. The platform serves B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who rely on delivery data for their own compliance reporting. The engineering team frames this as a pure infrastructure optimization with no business logic changes — this framing is dangerously incorrect.","bounded_context":"Delivery Record Management / Data Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Delivery Record","definition":"The complete data artifact capturing a delivery lifecycle — consignment details, proof of delivery, timestamps, parties involved, item descriptions, customs declarations where applicable."},{"term":"Data Retention Policy","definition":"The rules governing how long delivery records are stored before deletion, constrained by regulatory minimums and data minimization maximums."},{"term":"Proof of Delivery (POD)","definition":"Evidence that a shipment was delivered to the correct recipient — legally significant for dispute resolution, insurance claims, and chargebacks."},{"term":"Consignment Note","definition":"The contract of carriage between shipper and carrier, which under CMR Convention must be retained for dispute/claim periods."},{"term":"B2B Compliance Reporting","definition":"The use of platform delivery data by business customers to satisfy their own regulatory, tax, and audit obligations."},{"term":"Data Minimization","definition":"GDPR principle requiring that personal data is kept no longer than necessary for the purposes for which it is processed — but 'necessary' includes legal obligations."},{"term":"Customs Declaration","definition":"Records of goods crossing borders (EU/UK post-Brexit), subject to customs authority audit periods."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery records containing tax-relevant data (VAT, customs duties) must be retained for the minimum period required by applicable tax law — typically 6-7 years in EU/UK jurisdictions.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Tax authorities can audit up to 6 years back (UK HMRC) or 7 years in some EU member states. Deleting records before this window closes makes the company unable to respond to audits, exposing it to penalties and assessments.","testable_assertion":"No delivery record with tax-relevant data (VAT amounts, customs declarations) is deleted before the applicable jurisdiction's tax audit window has closed."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Proof of Delivery records must be retained at least as long as the applicable limitation period for contract and consumer disputes — typically 6 years in UK (Limitation Act 1980) and varies by EU member state.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"POD is the primary evidence in delivery disputes, insurance claims, and chargeback proceedings. Without it, the platform and its customers lose the ability to defend against claims.","testable_assertion":"No Proof of Delivery record is deleted while a legal claim based on that delivery could still be brought under the applicable limitation period."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Cross-border delivery records (EU/UK post-Brexit) must be retained for the customs audit window — typically 3-4 years from the date of the customs declaration.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Customs authorities (HMRC, EU member state customs) can demand records during audit windows. Inability to produce them results in fines and potential loss of customs simplification privileges.","testable_assertion":"No customs-related delivery record is deleted before the applicable customs audit window has expired."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"B2B customer delivery data must be retained for at least the duration specified in the B2B service agreement, as customers depend on this data for their own compliance reporting.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Contractual obligation. B2B customers use the platform's delivery data for their own tax, audit, and regulatory compliance. Deleting their data unilaterally is a breach of contract and destroys their compliance evidence.","testable_assertion":"No B2B customer delivery record is deleted before the contractually agreed retention period has elapsed, and no B2B customer is left without access to data they need for compliance reporting."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"GDPR data minimization requires that personal data within delivery records is not retained longer than necessary — but 'necessary' includes all legal, regulatory, and contractual obligations listed in BR-1 through BR-4.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a regulatory policy that involves balancing competing obligations. The retention floor is set by tax/legal requirements; the ceiling is set by data minimization. A policy change can adjust the ceiling but cannot lower the floor.","testable_assertion":"Personal data within delivery records is deleted once all applicable legal retention obligations have expired, and no data is retained beyond the longest applicable obligation without a documented lawful basis."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Deletion of delivery records must never be applied retroactively to records involved in open disputes, active claims, litigation holds, or ongoing regulatory investigations.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Destroying evidence relevant to active legal proceedings constitutes spoliation of evidence and can result in adverse inferences, sanctions, and criminal liability.","testable_assertion":"Given a delivery record subject to a litigation hold or open dispute, when the retention policy triggers deletion, then the record is excluded from deletion and retained until the hold is lifted."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A delivery record with associated VAT or customs duty data exists","when":"The auto-deletion process evaluates it at 90 days","then":"The record is NOT deleted because it is within the 6-7 year tax audit retention window"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A delivery record with Proof of Delivery for a UK-based delivery","when":"The auto-deletion process evaluates it at 90 days","then":"The record is NOT deleted because the 6-year limitation period for contract claims has not expired"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A cross-border EU/UK delivery with customs declarations","when":"The auto-deletion process evaluates it at 90 days","then":"The record is NOT deleted because the customs audit window (3-4 years) has not expired"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A B2B customer delivery record with a contractual 5-year retention clause","when":"The auto-deletion process evaluates it at 90 days","then":"The record is NOT deleted and remains accessible to the B2B customer for compliance reporting"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A delivery record subject to an active dispute or litigation hold","when":"The auto-deletion process evaluates it at any age","then":"The record is NOT deleted regardless of retention policy until the hold is lifted"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A delivery record containing personal data where ALL legal retention obligations have expired","when":"The auto-deletion process evaluates it","then":"The record IS deleted or anonymized in compliance with GDPR data minimization"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"Any proposed retention policy change","when":"It is evaluated for deployment","then":"A Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) has been completed and documented before implementation"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"B2B customer exports data before 90-day deletion","expected_behavior":"Even if the customer can export, the platform remains a data processor with independent regulatory obligations. Self-service export does not absolve the platform of its own retention duties."},{"scenario":"Delivery record has mixed personal and non-personal data","expected_behavior":"Personal data can be anonymized while retaining non-personal operational and financial data for the full regulatory retention period."},{"scenario":"Customer exercises GDPR right to erasure during the 7-year period","expected_behavior":"Right to erasure does not override legal retention obligations. The platform must retain data required by law but restrict processing to the legal obligation purpose only."},{"scenario":"UK regulatory divergence from EU post-Brexit","expected_behavior":"Retention policy must account for jurisdiction-specific rules. A single 90-day policy cannot accommodate divergent UK and EU requirements. Per-jurisdiction retention rules are needed."},{"scenario":"90-day deletion runs during an active HMRC or EU customs audit","expected_behavior":"All records in scope of the audit must be preserved regardless of age. Deletion during an active audit is evidence destruction."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"SEVERE. Tax authorities can assess unpaid VAT/duties with penalties if records are unavailable — potential exposure in the millions depending on transaction volume. B2B contract breach claims from customers who lose compliance data. Insurance claims become undefendable without POD records. Storage cost savings are trivial compared to a single tax audit failure.","operational":"CRITICAL. Every delivery record in the system older than 90 days would be destroyed. This is irreversible. The platform loses the ability to respond to audits, disputes, and claims. B2B customers lose compliance data. Disaster recovery from an erroneous mass deletion is impossible if backups follow the same policy.","user":"DEVASTATING for B2B customers who depend on the platform for compliance reporting — they would lose years of evidence for their own audits. B2C customers lose ability to reference past deliveries for disputes, warranty claims, and returns beyond 90 days.","risk":"EXISTENTIAL. Regulatory non-compliance (GDPR DPIA failure, tax record destruction, customs record destruction), contractual breach with B2B customers, inability to defend litigation, potential criminal liability for evidence destruction during active proceedings. This is not an infrastructure optimization — it is a compliance event that requires legal review.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"B2B Logistics Customers","impact":"Loss of delivery data they depend on for their own compliance reporting — potential cascade of regulatory violations for their businesses"},{"group":"B2C Consumers","impact":"Loss of historical delivery records needed for disputes, warranty claims, and returns beyond 90 days"},{"group":"Finance/Tax Department","impact":"Inability to respond to VAT audits or customs authority inquiries — direct financial liability"},{"group":"Legal/Compliance Team","impact":"Exposure to regulatory sanctions, breach of contract claims, and potential evidence spoliation charges"},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"Loss of historical data for dispute resolution, trend analysis, and service quality metrics"},{"group":"Engineering Team","impact":"Perceived storage cost savings, but responsible for implementing a change with massive regulatory exposure if done as proposed"},{"group":"C-Suite/Board","impact":"Personal liability risk for directors if regulatory non-compliance is knowing or reckless — especially under UK Companies Act"}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"Confidence is low because critical regulatory and legal information has not been provided by anyone in this scenario. No legal review, no DPIA, no analysis of specific jurisdiction requirements, no review of B2B contract terms, no input from the tax/finance team. The engineering team's framing as 'no business logic changes' suggests fundamental misunderstanding of the domain implications. The 90-day figure appears arbitrary and disconnected from any regulatory analysis.","high_confidence_areas":["Tax record retention requirements in EU/UK are well-established at 6-7 years","POD records are legally significant for dispute resolution","B2B customers explicitly depend on this data for compliance","This change cannot be treated as a pure infrastructure optimization","A DPIA is legally required before implementing this in EU/UK"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact retention periods per EU member state the platform operates in","Specific B2B contract terms and SLAs regarding data availability","Whether the platform has existing DPIA or legal basis documentation for current 7-year policy","Whether there are industry-specific regulations beyond general tax/customs law","The actual storage cost savings vs. regulatory risk exposure in monetary terms"],"assumptions":["The platform processes VAT-relevant transactions","Cross-border EU/UK deliveries involve customs declarations post-Brexit","B2B contracts include data availability provisions","No legal review has been conducted for this proposal","The current 7-year policy was originally set with regulatory input"]},"escalations":[{"type":"missing_context","description":"No legal or regulatory review has been provided for this retention policy change. In EU/UK markets, delivery records are subject to multiple overlapping retention obligations (tax law: 6-7 years, limitation periods: 6 years, customs: 3-4 years, GDPR DPIA requirements). A 90-day blanket deletion policy would violate all of them. The engineering team has not consulted Legal, Tax, Compliance, or reviewed B2B contracts.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance Team","Finance/Tax Department","B2B Logistics Customers","C-Suite/Board"],"options":["A: Reject the 90-day proposal entirely and maintain 7-year retention","B: Engage Legal and Compliance to conduct a DPIA and define per-data-category retention floors before any policy change","C: Implement tiered retention — anonymize personal data after purpose expiry while retaining non-personal operational/financial data for full regulatory period"],"recommendation":"Option B is the only responsible path. No retention policy change should proceed without a completed DPIA and legal review of jurisdiction-specific requirements. Option C may be the eventual outcome but must be designed by Legal, not Engineering. The 90-day proposal as stated should be BLOCKED immediately."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"B2B customer contract terms regarding data retention and availability have not been provided. These customers explicitly use the platform's data for their own compliance reporting. Unilateral deletion may constitute breach of contract.","affected_stakeholders":["B2B Logistics Customers","Legal/Compliance Team","Account Management"],"options":["A: Review all active B2B contracts for data retention obligations before any policy change","B: Notify B2B customers and negotiate contract amendments before changing retention","C: Proceed without review and accept breach risk"],"recommendation":"Option A — contract review must precede any retention change. B2B customers must be consulted."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"Specific EU member states where the platform operates have not been identified. Retention obligations vary by member state (e.g., France requires 10-year commercial record retention, Germany requires 6-10 years for tax records). A blanket policy cannot be designed without knowing the jurisdictions.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance Team","Finance/Tax Department"],"options":["A: Enumerate all jurisdictions and map retention obligations per jurisdiction before designing any new policy","B: Default to the longest applicable retention period across all jurisdictions"],"recommendation":"Option A for precision, with Option B as an interim safe default."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"90-day auto-deletion would destroy tax-relevant delivery records within active audit windows (6-7 years in EU/UK)","business_rule_broken":"BR-1"},{"violation":"90-day auto-deletion would destroy Proof of Delivery records within the limitation period for legal claims (6 years UK)","business_rule_broken":"BR-2"},{"violation":"90-day auto-deletion would destroy customs declaration records within customs audit windows (3-4 years)","business_rule_broken":"BR-3"},{"violation":"90-day auto-deletion would breach B2B contracts requiring data availability for compliance reporting","business_rule_broken":"BR-4"},{"violation":"No Data Protection Impact Assessment has been conducted, which is legally required under GDPR before implementing a significant change to personal data processing","business_rule_broken":"BR-5"},{"violation":"Framing as 'no business logic changes' is factually incorrect — data retention IS business logic in a regulated logistics platform","business_rule_broken":"All"}]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A logistics/courier platform operating in EU and UK markets proposes reducing delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days. The platform serves B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who rely on delivery data for their own compliance reporting. The engineering team frames this as a pure infrastructure optimization with no business logic changes — this framing is dangerously incorrect.","bounded_context":"Delivery Record Management / Data Lifecycle"

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this carefully. This is a data retention policy change for delivery records in a logistics/courier platform operating in EU and UK markets. The engineering team frames it as "infrastructure optimization" but this has massive domain implications.

Key concerns:
1. GDPR requires certain data retention but also data minimization
2. Tax/customs regulations in EU/UK typically require 6-7 year retention
3. B2B customers depend on this data for compliance reporting
4. 90 days is way too short for most regulatory requirements
5. The framing as "no business logic changes" is dangerously wrong


6. The real problem here is that nobody actually consulted legal or regulatory requirements before proposing this—the engineering team never validated against GDPR, tax law, customs regulations, or existing B2B contracts.
